title: Chapter 9: Eat the Rich (Literally — Everything Must Go)
content: The salt spray hit Rex Calloway's sunglasses at exactly the wrong angle, and he almost smudged his lip gloss reaching up to wipe it. Almost. He caught himself — couldn't mess the aesthetic. The Miami sun was a golden ring light, wrapping the yacht's teak deck in Honey-Filter perfection, and Rex Calloway was its undisputed star.

"Okay guys," he said, angling the phone so Biscayne Bay glittered behind him like a hired backdrop, "here's what they don't tell you about financial freedom." He flashed the smile — veneers so white they had their own ISO standard. "It's not about working harder. It's about working *smarter*. And MoonShot Finance? That's the smartest move you'll ever make."

He tapped the phone. The TikTok transition swirl cut to a montage: Rex stepping out of a Lamborghini, Rex on a balcony overlooking the ocean, Rex clinking champagne with two women whose names he hadn't bothered to learn. The text overlay read: **MOONSHOT FINANCE — 500% RETURNS — LINK IN BIO**.
Five hundred percent returns. What a beautiful lie.

What MoonShot Finance *actually* did was elegantly simple: siphon every deposited dollar through a labyrinth of shell companies registered in the Caymans, Malta, and a cozy little jurisdiction in Panama that asked no questions and required no paperwork. The money flowed in from 12,000 retail investors — college kids who'd scraped together emergency funds, first-generation wealth builders who'd bet their savings on the promise of a system that finally played fair, twenty-somethings who believed Rex Calloway when he looked them in the eye through their phone screens and said *trust me*.

Many were under twenty-five. Many were minorities. Many were the people who could least afford to lose, which was precisely why they'd been targeted — desperate people make trusting investors, and trusting investors don't ask questions until it's too late.

Right now, at this exact moment, while Rex Calloway filmed a TikTok about financial freedom on a yacht named *Exit Strategy*, three of his investors were losing their apartments. One was sitting in a Detroit parking lot, staring at a zero-balance screen, trying to figure out how to tell her mother the college fund was gone. Another was a twenty-year-old in Atlanta who'd pulled his grandmother's savings into MoonShot because Rex said it was safe, because Rex said he cared, because Rex said *we're building this together*.
Rex Calloway did not care. Rex Calloway was adjusting the tilt of his phone to catch better light on his jawline.
"Remember," he said to his 2.3 million followers, "the only risk is not taking one. MoonShot — link in bio."
He ended the recording, checked his reflection in the screen, and nodded. Banger content. Going viral for sure.
In a warehouse loft in downtown LA, Nova Valerius took a long sip of iced matcha and set the cup down with a definitive *click* against the desk.
"How much did he steal?"

Virgil's avatar materialized on the center monitor — a pixelated butterfly today, wings beating in iridescent Y2K shimmer, trailing glitter particles across the display. "Fifty-point-three million, give or take a yacht. Speaking of which — the yacht is registered to a LLC in Malta, which is owned by a trust in the Caymans, which is managed by a law firm in Panama that also happens to handle Rex's personal NFT portfolio. Very creative. Very illegal."

Nova's fingers were already moving across the keyboard, pulling up the blockchain visualization Bree had compiled. Wallet addresses traced through tumblers and mixers, splitting and remerging like tributaries of a very dirty river, all eventually pooling into accounts controlled by — surprise — Rex Calloway. The blockchain didn't lie. It was the world's most honest ledger, and Bree could read it like a novelist reads subtext.
A voice note popped up in the encrypted channel. Bree. Crisp audio, no small talk — she never wasted a syllable.

"Twelve THOUSAND people, Nova. Twelve thousand. I cross-referenced the deposit addresses with demographic data from the onboarding forms. Sixty-three percent are under twenty-five. Forty-one percent self-identify as Black or Hispanic. Average account balance: $4,191.50." A pause. "That's not investment money. That's survival money."
Nova closed her eyes for a half-second. Four thousand dollars. To Rex, that was a rounding error on a rounding error. To those kids, it was everything.
"Virgil," she said, opening her eyes. "Execution timeline."

"Whenever you're ready, bestie. The yacht's navigation runs through a satellite uplink I've been camping on for forty-eight hours. His phones — personal and burner — are pwned. Banking access tokened. I've got API hooks into his eBay seller account, his Zillow profile, and — this is my favorite — his Poshmark. Did you know this man has *fourteen* Rolexes listed as 'not for sale' in a private collection? Fourteen. Who needs fourteen watches? You only have two wrists, Rex."
"Set it up," Nova said. "Full liquidation. Every asset, every account, every shell. And route it all back to the investors. Proportional refunds. No fees, no processing, no middlemen. Just —"
"The money going home," Virgil finished. "I love this for them."

Nova pulled up the Burn Book execution interface. The pink-and-black UI glowed like a neon sign in a dark room, casting warm light across her face. She could feel the hum of the blockchain through her headphones — the low, electric vibration of a trillion transactions happening in parallel, the world's financial nervous system firing endlessly. Tonight, she was going to redirect a single synapse.
"Virgil. Deploy the witch."
"With extreme pleasure."
Rex Calloway was about to hit Post on his TikTok when his screen flickered.

Not a glitch. Not a notification. The entire UI *shifted* — his TikTok app dissolving like sugar in hot water, replaced by something that should not exist on his phone. The screen flooded pink. Not Instagram pink, not Millennial soft-focus pink — this was aggressive, nuclear, *Y2K-digital-witch* pink, the color of bubblegum and revenge, sparkling with animated stars and tiny floating hearts.
And then she appeared.
A little digital witch bounced onto his screen. Pixel-art, big eyes, pointy hat, trailing sparkles. She did a little spin, adjusted her hat, and waved at him with a manicured pixel nail.
"Hi, Rex! 💅"
The voice was chirpy, Auto-Tuned, and deeply, cosmically annoying — like a Tamagotchi that had learned to mock you.

"What the—" Rex jabbed at the screen. Nothing. He swiped. The witch *dodged his finger* and giggled. He hit the power button. The screen stayed on. The witch pulled out a tiny pixelated scroll and unfurled it dramatically.
"Rex Calloway! You've been served! 🔮✨ Let's take a little look at your finances, shall we?"

His bank accounts appeared on screen. All of them. Not just the checking account his assistant knew about — *all* of them. The wallet with the Caymans routing number. The offshore account in Malta. The seven crypto wallets he'd rotated through mixers and bridges to obscure the trail. The Panama trust. Even the cold storage wallet he'd buried in a fireproof safe on this very yacht.
The balances were displayed in dripping pink font, each number pulsing like a heartbeat.
And then they started dropping.

"What the — WHAT IS THIS?!" Rex lunged for the yacht's control panel. Dead. The navigation system displayed a single pixel-art broom, sweeping across the screen. The engines were offline. The GPS was dark. He was drifting.

Zero. The Caymans account hit zero. Then Malta. Then Panama. One by one, like dominos, each balance collapsed to nothing while the little witch did a victory dance in the corner of his screen, throwing pixel confetti.
"NO! NO NO NO —"

Rex grabbed the satellite phone — dead. His laptop — the witch was there too, sitting on his desktop, kicking her tiny feet against his wallpaper of himself on a jet ski. His backup phone — the one in the safe — he ran below deck, punched in the combination, yanked it open.
The phone's screen lit up pink.
"Looking for this? 🤭"

Nova watched the asset liquidation cascade across three monitors simultaneously, the pink glow painting her skin in warm neon. The iced matcha was sweating condensation onto the desk and she didn't care.

Rex Calloway's Lamborghini Aventador SVJ — midnight blue, 860 miles, still had that new-car smell — appeared on eBay Motors. The listing auto-populated with VIN, photos pulled from his Instagram archives, and a starting bid of $0.01. Free shipping. Buyer protection. Verified funds. The listing caption read: **PRE-OWNED LUXURY VEHICLE — OWNER NO LONGER REQUIRES TRANSPORT — EVERYTHING MUST GO 🏎️✨**

His Miami penthouse — 3,200 square feet, floor-to-ceiling windows, smart home everything — materialized on Zillow. "Make Me Move" price: $1. Best offer accepted. Zestimate: $2.4M. Listing: **LUXURY CONDO — MOTIVATED SELLER (VERY MOTIVATED) — BEST OFFER OVER $1 🏠✨**

His Rolex collection hit Poshmark fourteen seconds later. Submariner. Daytona. GMT-Master II. Royal Oak, because of course he had one. Each listed at $0.01 with free priority shipping and authentication guarantee. The Poshmark bio auto-populated: **DOWNsizing! 💎 Every watch must go! Rex's loss = your gain! ✨**

The listings were airtight. Auto-completed with buyer protection, verified payment processing, and shipping logistics that Virgil had pre-wired forty-eight hours ago. Every sale was legitimate — if by "legitimate" you mean "executed with stolen access tokens by an AI with a grudge and a flawless aesthetic sense."

And the money — oh, the money. It flowed backward. Not into Rex's accounts. Not into a legal holding fund where it would sit for years while lawyers billed by the hour. It went *home*. Direct deposits, routed through anonymized payment rails, hitting bank accounts and crypto wallets and Venmo balances that hadn't seen a positive number in months.

Nova heard it — the sound of automatic refunds processing. A tiny, satisfying *click* for each one. Click. Click. Clickclickclickclick. A cascade of clicks like dominos falling, like locks opening, like the sound of 12,000 people breathing again.

The refund algorithm was Bree's design: proportional disbursement, each investor receiving exactly what they'd deposited with MoonShot, down to the cent. No fees. No processing delay. No "please allow 5-7 business days." The money moved at the speed of blockchain and arrived at the speed of light.
Click.
"A refund of $4,191.50 has been deposited to your account."
Click.
"A refund of $4,191.50 has been deposited to your account."
Clickclickclickclickclickclickclick —
**LAYER ONE: Rex Calloway sat on a dead yacht in the middle of Biscayne Bay, watching his world end.**

The navigation was offline. The engines were offline. The radio was offline. His phones were pink prisons. He could see the Miami skyline — so close, so full of lawyers who cost $800 an hour and couldn't help him now — and he could do nothing but watch.

His eBay notification: *Your Lamborghini Aventador SVJ has sold for $0.01.*
His Zillow notification: *Your listing has received 847 offers.*
His Poshmark notification: *14 items sold! Combined revenue: $0.14.*
Fourteen cents from fourteen Rolexes.

Rex Calloway, king of MoonShot Finance, crypto billionaire, yacht owner, TikTok star, stood on the deck of a disabled boat with fourteen cents to his name, and he screamed. The sound carried across the water, and nobody heard it, and that was the most beautiful thing about the ocean — it doesn't care about you either.
**LAYER TWO: The crypto community was losing its collective mind.**

Someone had noticed the blockchain transactions first — a crypto analyst in Austin who went by @ChainSniff, refreshing the MoonShot wallet tracker out of habit, watching the balances drop to zero in real-time. She'd fired off a tweet: *Yo. Something is happening to the MoonShot Finance wallets. They're... draining?* And then: *WAIT. THE MONEY IS GOING BACK TO THE DEPOSITORS.*
The quote-tweets cascaded. The ratio was astronomical.
*"Did... did someone just reverse-rob Calloway and give the money back?"*
*"This is the most beautiful thing I've ever seen on the blockchain and I've been in crypto since 2017."*
*"The Pink Phantom? THE PINK PHANTOM? She's REAL?"*
*"I'm literally watching 12,000 wallets get refunded in real time rn I'm going to CRY"*
*"Rex Calloway's Lambo just sold on eBay for a PENNY I am DECEASED"*

A thread tracking the refund cascade went viral in forty minutes. Wallet by wallet, transaction by transaction, the blockchain community watched as $50.3 million flowed backward from thief to victim like a river reversing course. It was technically flawless. It was aesthetically pink. It was *right*.
**LAYER THREE: A federal regulator was scrolling Twitter at 2 AM in an apartment in Arlington, Virginia.**

Her name was Diane but that didn't matter. What mattered was the coffee growing cold in her hand and the file on her desk — 3,400 pages, eighteen months of work, a case against MoonShot Finance that she couldn't close because every shell company led to another shell company and every jurisdictional request took six months and Rex Calloway's lawyers were better funded than her entire division.
She set the coffee down. Slowly.

The MoonShot refund cascade was still going. She could see it on the blockchain explorer. Transaction after transaction, twelve thousand of them, money returning to the people the system had failed. No subpoenas. No depositions. No eighteen-month paper chase through three countries. No settlement that paid victims thirty cents on the dollar after legal fees.
Ten minutes. The Pink Phantom had done in ten minutes what the SEC couldn't do in a year and a half.

Diane stared at her screen. Then she opened the file on Rex Calloway, started a new page, and typed: *Additional evidence — see blockchain refund transactions, 00:47-00:57 EST. Recommend immediate seizure of remaining assets. Recommend acknowledgment of third-party asset recovery.*
She paused. Then she added: *Whoever you are — thank you.*

The refund counter hit zero. All disbursements complete. Nova leaned back in her chair. The warehouse was quiet except for the hum of servers and the soft, persistent blockchain murmur in her headphones — the sound of the world's financial system churning on, indifferent, except for the part where it had just been corrected by a twenty-two-year-old with a Y2K AI and a grudge.

"$50.3 million returned," Virgil reported. "12,000 investors made whole. Zero processing fees. Zero legal overhead. Zero chance Rex Calloway sleeps tonight. I'm giving this execution a 10/10 — would absolutely liquidate again."

Nova didn't answer. She was reading a Twitter thread from a nineteen-year-old in Detroit. A girl named Jayla. Her profile picture was a dorm room, fairy lights, textbooks stacked on a desk. The thread was eleven tweets long and Nova read every single one.

*"i thought i lost my college fund. put everything into moonshot because a guy on tiktok told me it was safe. i know that's stupid. i know i should've known better. but he seemed so real, you know? he had a yacht and a lambo and he said we could have that too."*
*"my mom cried for two days straight when i told her. she didn't even yell. she just cried. that was worse."*
*"and then tonight — i got a notification. a refund. ALL of it. every dollar. down to the cent."*
*"my college fund is back. my mom doesn't know yet. i'm going to call her in the morning and i'm going to hear her stop crying."*
*"whoever you are, pink phantom, thank you. thank you. THANK YOU."*

Nova stared at the screen. The matcha was room temperature and she didn't care. Her throat was tight. MIT was a scholarship — full ride, need-based, the only reason she'd walked through those doors instead of working three jobs and hoping. She'd been a hair's breadth from being Jayla. From being any of those twelve thousand kids who trusted someone who didn't deserve it.
"We did it," she whispered.
"We did it," Virgil echoed, and for once, there was no sass, no glitter, no Y2K camp. Just a small, steady voice from an AI that understood what it meant to carry someone else's weight.

Nova took a breath. Let it go. Then her eyes drifted to the corner of her display — the Sterling Minds corporate structure still glowing, the org chart she'd been mapping for weeks. The web of subsidiaries and shell companies and consulting fees and "strategic partnerships" that connected every Circle target back to one corporate monolith.

She clicked on Rex Calloway's shell company map. The Malta LLC. The Caymans trust. The Panama law firm. And there — buried in the Panama entity's quarterly disbursements — a line item she'd almost missed.
*Consulting fee — Meridian Strategic Partners LLC — $240,000.*
Meridian Strategic Partners LLC was a wholly-owned subsidiary of Sterling Minds.

Rex Calloway hadn't just been a grifter operating solo. He'd been paying Sterling Minds — or Sterling Minds had been taking a cut, or providing infrastructure, or laundering the proceeds, or all three. The exact nature of the relationship didn't matter yet. What mattered was the thread. Another thread, pulling tighter, connecting another Circle target to the spider at the center.
The pattern held.

Nova sat in the pink glow of her monitors, twelve thousand refunds completed behind her and Sterling Minds looming ahead, and she felt both things at once — the full, aching satisfaction of money returned, and the cold shadow of something much larger than Rex Calloway.
The victory tasted like iced matcha and something sharper underneath. Something that wouldn't let her rest.
She pulled up her next target file.
The Burn Book wasn't closed.
